topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The official notice, tacked haphazardly to the bulletin board in the common area, felt like a poorly drawn map leading to nowhere. I stared at the faded ink, tracing the outline of the final closure date with my finger, over and over. It was just… illogical. We had survived three years of late-night study sessions fueled by questionable instant ramen, we had navigated the labyrinthine bureaucracy of registration, and now this edifice, this supposed launchpad for my future, was simply dissolving. I walked over to the window, the glass cool against my forehead, and looked out at the sprawling campus, the familiar quad looking suddenly alien, like a stage set that had been abruptly dismantled mid-performance. My hands started to fidget, the edges of my textbooks suddenly feeling heavy and pointless. "This can't be right," I muttered to the empty room, the sound thin and brittle in the quiet air.

[story 2]

I kept re-reading the email, the same paragraph repeating itself in my mind, yet each time the words landed with a strange, hollow echo. It wasn't the sudden shock that held me; it was the sheer lack of a discernible reason. There were no budget cuts mentioned, no academic restructuring announced—just a final administrative decree. I paced my dorm room floor, my boots scuffing the cheap linoleum. I stopped by my desk, picked up a pen, and began to scribble frantic, nonsensical diagrams in the margins of my notes, trying to force a pattern where none existed. A knot tightened in my chest, a physical pressure that made it hard to draw a full breath. I leaned my forehead against the cool surface of my desk, closing my eyes, trying to filter the overwhelming sense of disorientation. What was the actual mechanism behind this shutdown? It refused to cohere.

[story 3]

When the dean finally called the mandatory assembly, the air in the auditorium felt thick, suffocating. Everyone else seemed to be absorbing the news with a sort of resigned sadness, but I felt completely detached, watching the proceedings as if I were observing a foreign film. I kept shifting my weight, my gaze darting from the speaker to the exit signs, searching for some hidden clue, some secret code embedded in the presentation. When the final announcement was made, the silence that followed wasn't one of acceptance; it was a vacuum. I stood up abruptly, my chair scraping loudly against the floor, and walked toward the exit, my steps oddly light, as if walking through water. I didn't know what to do with the sudden emptiness in my stomach, or why my palms were suddenly slick with sweat. The entire situation felt like a badly translated instruction manual.
